item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
intriguing concept by including the fact this historical-pillar includes the life of everyone with similar incidents to another SCP that tells you specifically how long you have until you die. Still good nonetheless.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I largely agree with Mortos and TL333S on this one. It's not a bad concept, but for me, the ending came so abruptly that it felt largely unearned. When something is implied to be this powerful and/or potentially dangerous, I like a better buildup to it than what was presented here. It seemed almost matter-of-factly stated that this object killed an O5 member with so few resulting ripples that a "welcome to the job" was thrown at the end. It's one thing to have a twist on an article that subverts expectations; it's quite another to suddenly throw in a huge change in focus with little to no buildup surrounding it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [JACKING OFF REDACTED]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the newer terminal type animation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine being an artist in a void. Everything you do gets to be or not be, as you decide. Nothing that is, is without your own revision.
Nothing that is, was ever seen by something other than you or the strange representation of your art, given life for seemingly no reason.
So you decide to show this weird existence a bit of your writing.

It starts reading it, in a slow manner, very carefully, from start to ???? (Present?) And a bit beyond.
Then it gets a rubber and starts trying to scrub something away, presumably to change it.

Rude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
20mm thick bulletproof glass

6 static cameras

Uber specific measurements like these usually bug me, and they still kind of do here. But I'm not so easily unhinged by 2 numbers that I can't enjoy the rest of the piece. Good shit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. Guess history, is written in stone.

The sass present in the narration here is quite entertaining, as is the final log with the implications for the SCP Foundation attempting to use the knowledge present on the object to alter the course of human events.

+1. Solid work, GJ.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This ended quite a bit sooner than I'd expected, but I can't really fault you for cutting to the chase. I guess it's just that, with all the visual ornaments and trappings, I'd expected a more expansive narrative. Still, there's some really enjoyable prose here (particularly the birth), and the ended was very nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
| DOES THE BLACK MOON HOWL?

If he feels like it, I guess.

hell yes i love it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed the ending of this (especially the particularly scathing nature of the commentary against the old O5-7), but the rest of I found pretty dry. The containment procedures seem massively overblown and largely unnecessary - I kept waiting for some big event or hook that would make them relevant and it never came. And honestly, I started skimming the accounts of peoples lives because they just weren't that interesting. Well written, certainly, but bland.

There's a lot of potential for things like this (an anomalous obelisk that contains infinite amounts of info is a good hook), and the revelation that it apparently has info on peoples entire lives even if they aren't dead yet is a nice one (I didn't pick that up from the earlier descriptions), but I feel like a lot more could be done with the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would mean the author is punishing characters for the actions he probably writes them doing. Unless they do retain some free will? I don't know, I think it needs something more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was engaging from beginning to end. I think the fate of O5-7 could be expanded upon, but it’s good as it is. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is Xenophon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I think that analysis of the criticism holds up. Or at least, it makes sense to me right now. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: Ah, damn. Meant to reply to TL333s, but whatever. Can't delete it now.

Like I said in another comment, I think the first addendum is mainly meant to hint at the fact that the priests being discussed have access to and the knowledge of the obelisk. People solicit them for help, namely the subject and his neighbor, but they are given none, and it is known that the priests have this knowledge, because the subject curses them for not helping.

I'd say that the "criticism" evolves as language evolves.

++The first subject
--The first subject

is a poor drunkard whose job is to make and sell pottery. Being unable to get pregnant with his wife may imply that one of them is sterile. The criticism comes more in form of the unnecessary acknowledgment of his drinking, and also the noting of his own shame in being unable to have children.

++The second subject
--The second subject

(who I'm going to take a shot and say is Xenophon) is a philosopher who is somewhat more deserving of leniency and I'd say is offered a tone that the man himself would agree with. As a more thoughtful, intelligent, and experienced person than the first, there's more to draw upon than simple actions. There's his belief system, those he associates with, and also the experience of being pitied and essentially having who he is diminished in comparison to others.

++The three more modern subjects and the O5
--The three more modern subjects and the O5

are more personal attacks because of how more modern language more simply enables the author to criticize them. There's by and large a more obvious tone of being looked down on and belittled by the author, but maybe that's what would most make each subject feel strongest about. Perhaps the tone the author writes in for each person is meant to affect that person the most should they read their own history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the point is that the author is an omni-present, timeless being who literally narrates the fabric of time - effectively a god. The O5 was trying to use this knowledge and break the narrative, which the author clearly didn't like.

The first addendum speaks of priests, who are clearly privy to the knowledge of the baselisk. It isn't said that they help anyone who asks for it - perhaps that man's daughter is destined to remain sick and die. Perhaps the subject's wife is doomed to be sterile, and that his pottery were going to shatter. The priests don't intervene, perhaps because they know of the danger of doing so, but at the same time are able to harmlessly speak of the future, because they can "recall" it without doing anything to affect it.

I'd say that the author simply has the power to do whatever they want to to people who try to violate the already-written narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall, I like the object, and I'm really down with those intensely complex containment procedures, especially for something classed Safe, but it starts to get dense and drag on once the excerpts show up, and I honestly had to wonder why they were all there. The ones near the end, that involve modern day people, make sense, but why the first few? And why aren't they as critical about their subjects as those following? Upvote after the contest, probably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it overall but the ending didn't do it for me. I think it's implied the O5 was kidnapped/punished by the author which doesn't make sense to me or really connect with anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nicely done. You had me engaged from the containment procedures to the final warning notice. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty simple but +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I told ya on discord: this has some problems with the presentation (I feel it may take a little long to hook the reader in) but the snippets from people's lives are extraordinarily well done and deserve an upvote all on their own. Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Self-upvoting has not been against the rules for about two years now.

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
With sincere thanks to Modern_Erasmus, Bluedanoob, Jeremiah Cimmerian, and everyone else who provided amazing feedback for this entry.

edit: Thank you to NewyRose for the awesome animated login text!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did. Why on earth wouldn't I?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You self-upvoted.
